description: Standard: cut a 400-word passage to 250 words

input.preserve: The opening sentence and the quote from the union representative
input.text:
The factory's closure announcement came without warning on a Tuesday morning in March, catching both workers and local officials off guard. More than 600 employees arrived for their shifts only to find security guards at the gates and a printed notice taped to the entrance doors. The notice, which was also emailed to staff at 6:00 AM, stated that operations would cease immediately and that all positions were being eliminated effective April 30.

The company blamed declining demand and rising costs, a familiar explanation in a region that has lost more than 12,000 manufacturing jobs in the past decade. Industry analysts noted that the company had recently invested $40 million in automation at a newer facility in another state — a detail the announcement did not mention.

Union representative Carlos Gutierrez called the closure 'a betrayal of every commitment this company made when it took $8 million in tax incentives to stay here ten years ago.' Gutierrez said the union would explore legal options related to the WARN Act, which requires 60 days' notice for mass layoffs. The company maintains it complied with the law by providing 60 days of paid notice from the announcement date.

Local officials scrambled to respond. The mayor convened an emergency meeting with economic development agencies and announced a job fair for affected workers within two weeks. The state's rapid response team was deployed to the facility to begin processing unemployment claims. Economists estimate the closure will reduce local tax revenue by approximately $2.4 million annually.
The new policy, which was announced on Tuesday by the city's director of transportation, will require all downtown parking meters to accept mobile payment starting in January 2027.

The European Green Deal, first proposed by Commission President Ursula von der Leyen in December 2019, represents the EU's most ambitious climate policy framework to date. The deal aims to make Europe the first climate-neutral continent by 2050, a target that has been enshrined in the European Climate Law passed in June 2021. The legislative package encompasses more than a dozen individual regulations and directives, collectively known as the Fit for 55 package, because they aim to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by at least 55 percent from 1990 levels by the year 2030.

The deal covers virtually every sector of the economy: energy production, transport, buildings, agriculture, and heavy industry. Key provisions include an expansion of the EU Emissions Trading System to cover maritime transport and buildings, a Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism to prevent carbon leakage from imports, and binding national targets for renewable energy deployment. Critics, particularly from central and eastern European member states, argue that the transition timeline is too aggressive and disproportionately burdens coal-dependent economies. Poland, which generates approximately 70 percent of its electricity from coal, has been the most vocal opponent.
